chunk_summary: Tele-nutrition for microcytic anemia requires careful differentiation of iron deficiency and hemoglobinopathies using indices, severity classification, and response checkpoints to ensure safe supplementation and timely escalation.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of tele-nutrition in managing anemia and micronutrient deficiencies.
Learn to differentiate iron deficiency anemia from thalassemia trait using indices like MCV, RDW, and Mentzer index.
Identify triggers for in-person evaluation in severe anemia or cardiopulmonary compromise.
Establish response checkpoints for iron supplementation and manage non-response effectively.
key_facts: Microcytosis with RDW >13 suggests iron deficiency, while Mentzer index <13 suggests thalassemia trait.
Severe anemia or cardiopulmonary symptoms require urgent in-person evaluation, not remote management.
Expected hemoglobin rise in iron deficiency anemia is ~1 g/dL within 14 days; lack of response warrants further investigation.
Hemoglobin cutoff tables should distinguish programmatic thresholds (e.g., MoHFW) from WHO reference cutoffs for comparability.
India has a high prevalence of hemoglobinopathies and mixed anemia, necessitating careful interpretation before intensifying iron supplementation.
reference_quote: WHO guideline on haemoglobin cutoffs to define anaemia (2024): 'Severe anemia thresholds require urgent in-person evaluation to prevent cardiopulmonary compromise.'
